coredata.dc:title: Recognition and nanocatalytic amplification of binary MXene carbon dot surface molecularly imprinted nanoprobe for determination of thiamethoxam by molecular spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Bai, Hongyan
Wen, Guiqing
Liang, Aihui
Jiang, Zhiliang
coredata.dc:description: In this experiment, using MXene quantum dot (MQD) as the core and thiamethoxam (TMX) as the template, a dual-functional surface molecularly imprinted polymer nanoprobe (MQD@MIP) for detecting of TMX was synthesized by microwave digestion procedure. And MQD@MIP was characterized in detail. It was found that this nanoprobe had dual function, which not only could specifically recognize TMX, but also could strongly catalyze the new indicator reaction of HAuCl4-maleic acid to produce gold nanoparticles (AuNPs). The AuNPs exhibit strong surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), resonance Rayleigh scattering (RRS) and surface plasmon resonance absorption (Abs) effect. Based on this, a new SERS/RRS/Abs trimode biosensoring platform was constructed for the determination of TMX, with a linear range of 1.0–150, 1.0–150, and 5.0–125 nmol/L TMX, and detection limit of 0.57 nmo/L, 0.84 nmol/L and 3.5 nmol/L, respectively. As a comparison, we used carbon dots (CDs) as the core to prepare surface molecularly imprinted polymers (CD@MIP) nanoprobe, and investigated the catalytic effect on the nanogold indicator reaction and the specific recognition effect on TMX.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-function nanosurface molecularly imprinted polymer
Thiamethoxam
Nanogold indicator reaction
Nanocatalytic amplification
SERS/RRS/Abs trimode biosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: SERS spectra of nanocatalysis and analysis system
RRS spectrum of nanocatalysis and analysis system
Abs spectra of nanocatalysis and analysis system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analytical principle of trimode molecular spectral detection of TMX based on MQD@MIP nanoprobe catalyzed nanogold reaction.

SERS spectra of nanocatalysis and analysis system (number of measurements n = 3). A: 50 μg/mL HAuCl4-100 μmol/L HCl-0.75 g/L MA-MQD@MIP; B: 50 μg/mL HAuCl4-100 μmol/L HCl-0.75 g/L MA-MQD; C: 50 μg/mL HAuCl4-100 μmol/L HCl-0.75 g/L MA-MIP; D: 1.0 μg/mL MQD@MIP-50 μg/mL HAuCl4-100 μmol/L HCl-0.75 g/L MA-TMX; E: 1.0 μg/mL MIP-50 μg/mL HAuCl4-100 μmol/L HCl-0.75 g/L MA-TMX; F: 1.0 μg/mL CD@MIP-50 μg/mL HAuCl4-100 μmol/L HCl-0.75 g/L MA-TMX.

RRS spectra nanocatalysis and analysis system (n = 3). A: 50 μg/mL HAuCl4-100 μmol/L HCl-0.75 g/L MA-MQD@MIP; B: 50 μg/mL HAuCl4-100 μmol/L HCl-0.75 g/L MA-MQD; C: 50 μg/mL HAuCl4-100 μmol/L HCl-0.75 g/L MA-MIP; D: 1.0 μg/mL MQD@MIP-50 μg/mL HAuCl4-100 μmol/L HCl-0.75 g/L MA-TMX; E: 1.0 μg/mL MIP-50 μg/mL HAuCl4-100 μmol/L HCl-0.75 g/L MA-TMX; F: 1.0 μg/mL CD@MIP-50 μg/mL HAuCl4-100 μmol/L HCl-0.75 g/L MA-(2.5–150 nmol/L) TMX.
UV-Abs spectra of nanocatalysis and analysis system (n = 3).
MIP recognition and catalytic amplification-trimode spectral analytical characteristics for detection of TMX.

head.ce:title: Recognition and nanocatalytic amplification of binary MXene carbon dot surface molecularly imprinted nanoprobe for determination of thiamethoxam by molecular spectroscopy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Ecology of Rare and Endangered Species and Environmental Protection (Guangxi Normal University), Ministry of Education

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this experiment, using MXene quantum dot (MQD) as the core and thiamethoxam (TMX) as the template, a dual-functional surface molecularly imprinted polymer nanoprobe (MQD@MIP) for detecting of TMX was synthesized by microwave digestion procedure. And MQD@MIP was characterized in detail. It was found that this nanoprobe had dual function, which not only could specifically recognize TMX, but also could strongly catalyze the new indicator reaction of HAuCl4-maleic acid to produce gold nanoparticles (AuNPs). The AuNPs exhibit strong surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), resonance Rayleigh scattering (RRS) and surface plasmon resonance absorption (Abs) effect. Based on this, a new SERS/RRS/Abs trimode biosensoring platform was constructed for the determination of TMX, with a linear range of 1.0–150, 1.0–150, and 5.0–125 nmol/L TMX, and detection limit of 0.57 nmo/L, 0.84 nmol/L and 3.5 nmol/L, respectively. As a comparison, we used carbon dots (CDs) as the core to prepare surface molecularly imprinted polymers (CD@MIP) nanoprobe, and investigated the catalytic effect on the nanogold indicator reaction and the specific recognition effect on TMX.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) have been used in optical and electrical analysis because they contain three-dimensional holes that are highly consistent with template molecules in spatial structure and functional group arrangement, and have good memory effect on template molecules and high selectivity [1,2]. Sergeyeva et al. [3] synthesized MIPs in situ using the technique of molecular imprinting combined with the semi-interpenetrating polymer networks. And a smartphone-based fluorimetric sensor system for the detection of aflatoxin B1 was developed with the range of 20–100 ng/mL, and the detection limit (DL) of 20 ng/mL. Tabanico et al. [4] fabricated an electrochemical sensor based on the use of screen-printed gold electrodes modified with MIPs, which was synthesized by electro-polymerization, and 0.01–0.1 mM enrofloxacin was detected by square wave voltammetry, with a DL of 0.02 mM. Mulyasuryani et al. [5] developed a MIP electrochemical sensor by modifying the screen-printed carbon electrode with chitosan-based MIP for the determination of 0.5–35 µM p-aminophenol with a DL of 3.6 ± 0.1 µM. The MIP was made from chitosan, cross-linker of glutaraldehyde and sodium tripolyphosphate, and the template molecule. However, the traditional MIP imprinting holes were buried too deep, and the template molecules were difficult to elute, which made the recognition rate limit. To overcome these disadvantages of MIP, nanosurface molecularly imprinted polymer (SMIP) technology was developed. It does not need grinding, screening and other processes. The mechanical strength is high, and the identification point is not easy to be damaged. With a large specific surface area, the template molecules can be almost completely removed, which maximizes the proportion of effective binding sites. The template molecules can easily access the molecular imprinting sites of the materials, thus showing fast binding dynamics. The commonly used nano-matrix materials of SMIP include gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) [6] and Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles [7]. Chen et al. [8] prepared a hollow MIP by self-polymerization of dopamine on the silica matrix, which was used to specifically identify horseradish peroxidase (HRP) in biological samples, and successfully applied to enrich HRP from complex sample matrix. Özkan et al. [9] constructed a surface plasmon resonance (SPR) nanosensor based on core-shell nanoparticles of hexagonal boron nitride nanosheets-MIP for the detection of anticancer drugs etoposide. As a new type of carbon nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have good solubility in water, less toxic and many kinds. CD as a substrate gives the SMIPs new properties. Kazemifard et al. [10] stabilized the silica shell produced by tetraethoxylsilane on the surface of CDs, and the fluorescence intensity of the prepared CDs@SiO2@MIPs increased with increasing of thiabendazole concentration. The MIP was coated on silica-carbon quantum dots (SiCQDs) to form MIP@SiCQDs using a sol-gel method, and a linear relationship between the fluorescence quenching effect and increased indoxacarb concentration could be determined for the range of 4–102 nM [11]. Compared with the above nanomaterials, quantum dots (QDs) derived from two-dimensional MXene materials have characteristics of better dispersion, easier functionalization, and stronger catalytic activity [12], which have attracted considerable attention. Wang et al. [13] synthesized Ti3C2Tx MXene quantum dots (MQDs) by reflux approach based on the cutting effect of tetramethylammonium hydroxide on Ti3C2Tx at high temperature, which showed excellent sensitivity and selectivity in the determination of Fe3+. Jiang et al. [14] synthesized N-MQDs via simple hydrothermal method with high electrochemical luminescence quantum efficiency, and established a method for the determination of mucin 1. As far as we know, there are no reports about MQD@MIP molecular spectral nanoprobe with dual-function of recognition and catalytic amplification.

While the sensitivity of conventional Raman scattering is low, surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) can greatly enhance the Raman scattering signal, and its sensitivity is greatly improved, reaching the single molecule level [15]. Liu et al. [16] prepared magnetic MIP microspheres fabricated by coating a layer of MIP on Fe3O4@Ag for SERS-based sensing, and it was applied for the rapid and label-free SERS detection of sibutramine, with a low DL of 1.0 × 10−9 M. Ren et al. [17] obtained the sandwiched Ag-TiO2@MIP by surface molecularly imprinting approach as SERS active substrate for minimum detection concentration of sulfadimethacil as low as 3.6 × 10−9 mol/L. Resonance Rayleigh scattering (RRS) is a kind of elastic light scattering, which can be used for drug detection [18], food detection [19], and ion detection [20] with simplicity, speedy and sensitivity. At present, there are no reports about the application of SMIP for RRS analysis. The Abs spectroscopy based on nano plasma resonance has been widely used due to its low cost, simple operation and speedy, but its sensitivity is low. Due to the characteristics of single-mode method, the detection method may have some problems such as low sensitivity, poor stability, poor reproducibility and high cost. The dual-mode and trimode methods can overcome these disadvantages and provide a variety of mode options. The coupling RRS with SERS not only has the sensitivity of SERS method, but also retains the precision of RRS method [21], which can provide two methods for selection. Catalytic amplification is an important way to improve the sensitivity. Nano catalysts are favored by researchers because of their simplicity, easy availability, high catalysis, good stability and diversity. Huang et al. [22] found that liquid crystal nanoparticles (LCNPs) could catalyze the reduction of HAuCl4 by NaH2PO2 to generate AuNPs, and a method was established for an aptamer-regulated LCNPs catalytic amplification-Abs detection of oxytetracycline. Wang et al. [20] found that CDAu could strongly catalyze the reaction of HAuCl4-fructose to generate AuNPs. Using AuNPs as a nanoprobe, a SERS/RRS/Abs trimode method was established for detection of trace As3+, based on the aptamer-regulated CDAu catalysis. Therefore, it is of great significance to combine the recognition and catalytic amplification combined SMIP nanoprobes with SERS/RRS/Abs spectroscopy to construct a new method for detection of Thiamethoxam (TMX).

TMX is a second-generation nicotinic insecticide with high efficiency and low toxicity. It is mainly used to control stinging insects and has lethal effect on aquatic and terrestrial invertebrates. And some people may have vertigo, headache, vomiting and muscle weakness and other symptoms after exposure to neonicotinoids [23]. Therefore, the development of new methods for detection of TMX with high sensitivity and selectivity are of great significance to the ecological environment and human health. At present, the detection methods of neonicotinoid residues have been concentrated on traditional large instruments, and the main detection methods include high performance liquid chromatography [24], high performance liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry [25] and gas chromatography [26]. However, these methods are costly, time-consuming, and require complex sample preparation, and the detection effect could not meet people's needs, so the application was limited. Therefore, researchers are constantly seeking simple, highly sensitive and selective detection methods. Kong et al. constructed an enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay that could rapidly detect TMX as low as 1.67 ± 0.12 nmol/L [27]. Fang et al. [28] built a ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer to detect TMX, with a DL of 4.11 nmol/L. In this study, based on the dual function integration MQD@MIP with catalytic amplification and specific recognition, a simple, fast and sensitive trimode sensing platform for SERS/RRS/Abs was developed to detect TMX.

A DXR Smart Raman spectrometer with a laser wavelength of 633 nm, a laser power of 3.0 mW, a slit of 25 µm, and an acquisition time of 5.0 s (Thermo Fisher Scientific Co., Waltham, USA), a model of F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi Co., Tokyo, Japan) with voltage of 350 V, and excited slit = emission slit of 5 nm, a dual-beam UV−visible spectrophotometer with a model of TU-1901 (Beijing Puxi General Equipment Limited Co., Beijing, China), a Themofisher Talos 200 s transmission electron microscope (Thermo Scientific Company, Waltham, USA) were used.

50 μg/mL HAuCl4 (Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company, Beijing, China) was used. Maleic acid (MA), thiamethoxam (TMX), α-methacrylic acid (α-MAA), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA), 2,2′-azodiisobutyronitrile (AIBN) were purchased from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd., (Shanghai, China). All reagents were of analytical grade, and the water was double distilled.

The preparation of MXeneTi3C2 was from our previous work [29]. In a 50 mL conical flask, 24 mg MXeneTi3C2 powder was dissolved in 30 mL water, and evenly mixed by ultrasonic for 1 h. Then, adjust the pH value of the mixture to∼9.0 with NH3·H2O. Then the solution was transferred to a microwave digestion apparatus at 160 ℃ for 2 h. After the reaction was completed, it was cooled to room temperature and centrifuged for 10 min at 10,000 r/min. Then, the obtained light- yellow solution was filtered by 0.22 µm microporous membrane and placed in refrigerator for use. The concentration of MQD solution was 80 μg/mL (calculated by MXene concentration).

MQD@MIP: 73 mg (0.25 mmol) TMX, 8 mL 0.8 mg/mL MQD, 85 μL α-MAA (1 mmol) and 30 mL 1 % PVA solution were added into 50 mL conical flask, the mixture was ultrasonicated for 30 min to form prepolymerization solution. Then, 1.89 mL (10 mmol) EGDMA was added and the mixture ultrasonicated for 50 min, and finally 20.7 mg AIBN was added. The reactants were transferred to a microwave digestion apparatus at 70 ℃ for 2 h. TMX removal from polymers took place by 10 mL of the eluent with methanol/acetic acid (9:1, v/v) and centrifuged for 10 min at 10,000 r/min three times. The Abs value at a wavelength of 310 nm was recorded to validate the complete removal of TMX. Finally, the product was dried in an oven at 40 ℃ for 24 h, and the white MQD@MIP product was obtained. In comparison, non-imprinted polymers (MQD@NIP) were synthesized in the same way without the addition of TMX.

The operation was same as above, but the eluent was water, and the obtained product was denoted as MQD@MIPTMX. Similarly, the MIP prepared under the same conditions without matrix MQD, and the product was washed off the template molecule three times with 10 mL water to obtain the product as MIPTMX.

In a 5.0 mL stoppered test tube, a certain amount of TMX, and 200 μL 10 μg/mL MQD@MIP were added, then left it in a water bath at 35 ℃ for 15 min. Subsequently, 100 μL 0.1 % HAuCl4, 200 μL 1 mmol/L HCl, 150 μL 10 g/L MA were added and diluted to 2 mL. The reaction solution was placed in a constant temperature water bath at 75 ℃ and heated for 13 min. Finally, the tube was cooled to room temperature, and then 100 μL 10−5 mol/L VB4r was added for SERS detection. The SERS intensity at 1617 cm−1 (I1617 cm−1) and a blank (I1617 cm−1)0 without TMX were recorded. The ΔII1617 cm-1-(I1617 cm-1)0 was calculated.

In the presence of NH3·H2O, MXeneTi3C2 nano sheets were cut and high fluorescence MQD was prepared by simple microwave digestion method. After ultrasonic treatment, TMX and MAA formed a prepolymer through hydrogen bond. Then, after adding crosslinking agent and initiator, the core-shell MQD@MIP nanoprobe was synthesized by microwave irradiation with the dual-function of catalytic activity and TMX-specific recognition. When there was no catalyst in the HAuCl4-MA system, a small amount of AuNP was generated. Because of its smaller particle size, MQD had a stronger catalytic effect as a catalyst, and the system generated more AuNPs. AuNPs have strong RRS and Abs peaks at 370 nm and 550 nm, respectively. After adding VB4r probe molecule, AuNPs show a strong SERS signal peak at 1617 cm−1. When MQDs were encapsulated by MIP, the core-shell structure MQD@MIP nanoprobe was formed, with a large particle size. However, in the presence of TMX, template molecule-specific recognition sites on the surface of MQD@MIP would be occupied by TMX, forming MQD@MIP-TMX complex, which hindered the contact between internal MQD and external indicative reaction system. Thus, the catalytic activity of MQD@MIP nanoenzyme was inhibited, the generation of AuNP was reduced, and SERS/RRS/Abs signal of the system was decreased. Therefore, a SERS/RR/Abs trimode sensing analysis platform with high sensitivity and selectivity for detecting TMX can be established ( Fig. 1).

To reveal the optical properties of the prepared materials, the spectral characteristics were studied (Fig. S1A-J). To verify the prepared MQD/MQD@MIP surface functional groups, studies were conducted using Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR) (Fig. S1K). The Abs peak of MQD at 3430 cm−1 was attributed to —OH stretching vibration, CO stretching vibration at 1650 cm−1, C—H stretching vibration at about 1440 cm−1 and C—O—C stretching vibration at 1100 cm−1 [30]. And the vibration at 3120 cm−1 was attributed to —NH groups, indicating that the surface of MQDs were passivated by —NH groups [30]. In particular, the vibration of the Ti-O bond may be the peak at 665 cm−1, and it is reported that most Ti atoms would be etched at higher temperatures [30]. Compared with MQD, the peak at 2982 cm−1 was attributed to the C—H stretching of sp3 carbon, at 1719 cm−1 attributed to the CO stretching of MAA and EGDMA, at 1450 cm−1 attributed to the C—H asymmetric deformation; the peak at 1151 cm−1 attributed to the axial deformation of C—O—C of EGDMA, and the peak at 946 cm−1 is attributed to the C-C stretching [31]. Another important information that can be obtained from FTIR spectrum is that there is no typical Abs band of TMX between 1500 and 1600 cm−1, attributed to the existence of NO2 group, which proves that the process of removing template molecules is effective [31]. In addition, due to the same synthesis step, the IR spectrogram of MQD@NIP was similar to that of MQD@MIP. In addition, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum (Fig. S1L), and X-ray electron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. S2) were studied respectively.

The prepared MQD, MIP and MDQ@MIP were scanned by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Many uniformly dispersed particles in the MQD sample could be observed from the figure ( Fig. 2A). The size distribution was calculated by counting 200 particles of MQDs in different regions, and the transverse size of MQD was directly measured from TEM images. The average transverse size of the sample was 3.71 nm, and the size of the sample was in the range of 1–7 nm (Fig. 2B). It can be seen from the SEM in Fig. 2C that MQDs are a large number of nanoparticles with uniform particles. It can be seen from SEM (Fig. 2D) of MQD@MIP that it had a spherical structure with lots of mesh molecular imprinting sites on its surface. According to the TEM of Fig. 2E, the MQD@MIP was spherical and coated with a thin translucent polymer layer, with a particle size of about 800 nm, while the surface of MIP was smooth microspheres with a particle size of about 1.5 µm (Fig. 2F). For the analytical system, when there was no TMX in the system, the AuNPs generated in the system were large and clustered (Fig. 2G). When TMX was added, TMX entered imprint holes on the surface of MQD@MIP, which reduced the catalytic activity of MQD@MIP. And the AuNPs generated in the system were rarely dispersed with a particle size of about 50 nm (Fig. 2H). In addition, from the corresponding energy spectrum, we can see that the five characteristic peaks of Au element are at 2.13, 9.71, 11.4, 11.6 and 13.4 keV respectively (Fig. 2I), which can prove the generation of AuNPs in the system.

A: TEM of MQDs (inset: photographs of MQDs under visible light and 365 nm UV lamp); B: size distribution histograms of MQDs; C: SEM of MQDs; D: SEM of MQD@MIP; E: TEM of MQD@MIP; F: TEM of MIP; G: TEM of 1.0 μg/mL MQD@MIP-50 μg/mL HAuCl4-100 μmol/L HCl-0.75 g/L MA; H: TEM of (E)−100 nmol/L TMX; I: EDS of (G and H).

A particle size analyzer was used to study the particle size distribution and Zeta potential of the materials (Fig. S3). And we also investigated the specific surface area and porosity (Fig. S4), and stability (Fig. S5). At the same time, the reproducibility of MQD@MIP was also examined. The reproducibility of the same batch of MQD@MIP was tested by measuring the SERS/RRS/Abs signal strength of 1.0 μg/mL MQD@MIP for the nanogold indicator reaction five times. The relative standard deviations (RSD) are 5.93 %, 4.57 %, and 1.98 %, respectively. Moreover, five different MQD@MIPs were prepared to measure the trimode signal strength of 1.0 μg/mL MQD@MIP for the nanogold indicator reaction. The RSD are 5.55 %, 4.32 % and 2.27 %, respectively. The results indicate that the nanoprobe has excellent reproducibility between batches.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SERS spectra of nanocatalysis and analysis system
ce:section.ce:para: Under the condition of 75 ℃ water bath, it was found that HAuCl4 was reduced by MA slowly to generate AuNPs, and the amount of generated AuNPs was less. When MQD@MIP/MQD/MIP/MQD@NIP/MQD@MIPTMX/MIPTMX was added to the system, all of them could obviously catalyze the reaction to generate AuNPs. After adding VB4r probe, the system produced strong SERS peaks at 1195 cm−1, 1395 cm−1 and 1617 cm−1. Among them, the strongest SERS peak was at 1617 cm−1, and the change of SERS signal of I1617 cm-1 had a linearly increasing relationship with the nanocatalyst concentration ( Fig. 3A–C, Fig. S6A-F). As had been proven, MQDs were the smallest in particle size (Fig. 2A), with their maximum catalytic slope of KMQD= 11419, which meant that the catalytic effect was the strongest [29]. MQD@MIP was a surface molecularly imprinted nanospheres with a core-shell structure based on MQD as its core. The molecular imprinted sites were located on the surface of the nanomaterials. After TMX elution, a partial of MQDs were exposed, so MQD@MIP also had strong catalytic ability (KMQD@MIP=4215.3). For MIP, its particle size was larger than MQD@MIP (Fig. 2), and there was no exposed substrate, so its catalysis was weaker (KMIP=631.9). As a nanoprobe without molecularly imprinted sites, the catalytic slope of MQD@NIP was K= 490.5. To MQD@MP-TMX and MIP-TMX complex, due to the presence of TMX, all of them had a certain catalytic effect, with catalytic slopes of KMQD@MIOTMX= 1157.1 and KMIPTMX= 389.8, respectively. The catalytic effect of CD/CDN/CD@MIP on the system was also investigated (Fig. S6D-F).

When TMX was added in the catalytic system of HAuCl4-HCl-MA-MQD@MIP, TMX was adsorbed on the molecular imprinting sites in the surface of MQD@MIP nanoprobes to form MQD@MIP-TMX complex which hindered the contact between the internal MQD and the external indicative reaction system. Thus, the catalytic activity of MQD@MIP nanoenzyme was inhibited, the AuNPs were reduced, and SERS signal of the system was reduced (Fig. 2D). For MQD@NIP, since there was no molecular imprinted site, MQDs were completely wrapped inside SMIP, so it only shows weak catalysis, with a slope of KMQD@NIP= 490.5 (Fig. S6A). After TMX was added, SERS signal of the system was not affected (Fig. S6G). Due to the limitations of MIP, it had certain limitations on the recognition rate of TMX (Fig. 2E). Compared with MIP, the adsorption capacity of TMX was stronger due to the large number of molecular immobilization sites located on the surface of MQD@MIP nanomaterials. Within a certain range, with the increase of TMX concentration, SERS signal showed a significant linear decrease. Therefore, a new SERS sensing analysis platform for detecting TMX was established. However, MQD@MIPTMX and MIPTMX only eluted a limited number of TMX because of the water as eluent, resulting in few MQDs nucleus leaking out, so they showed a catalytic effect (Fig. S6B, C). After the same amount of TMX was added, the SERS signal of the system decreased slightly (Fig. S6H, I). Meanwhile, the specific recognition effect of CD@MIP on TMX was also investigated, which could also be used for SERS to determine TMX (Fig. 3F). The standard deviations of the inserted figure were determined based on three parallel measurements (n = 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: RRS spectrum of nanocatalysis and analysis system
ce:section.ce:para: Under experimental conditions, AuNPs generated by the nanogold indicator reaction have two RRS peaks at 370 nm and 550 nm, among which the peak at 370 nm is more sensitive, so it is used for subsequent studies. In the experiment, it was found that with the increase of MQD@MIP/MQD/MIP/MQD@NIP/MQD@MIPTMX/MIPTMX/CD/CDN/CD@MIP concentration, the content of generated AuNP increased and the RRS intensity was enhanced accordingly ( Fig. 4 A-C, Fig. S7A-F). Similar to SERS spectra, the maximum slope of MQD was KMQD= 65545.5. However, after the addition of TMX, the same catalytic activity of MQD@MIP was inhibited, the content of AuNPs generated and the RRS signal was reduced in the system (Fig. 4D). Within a certain concentration range, with the increase of TMX concentration, the RRS signal of the system gradually decreases, according to which a new RRS sensing analysis platform for detecting TMX could be established. For MIP, the RRS signal gradually decreased with the increase of TMX (Fig. 4E). Similar to the SERS, the RRS signal of MQD@NIP changed barely after addition of TMX (Fig. S7G). For the MQD@MIPTMX and MIPTMX nanomaterials, the RRS signal decreased also after the addition of TMX (Fig. S7H, I). Using CD@MIP as probe and catalyst, the RRS signal decreased as TMX increased (Fig. 4F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Abs spectra of nanocatalysis and analysis system
ce:section.ce:para: The AuNPs generated by this system have a SPR Abs peak at 550 nm. when the generated AuNP increased, the value of ∆A550 nm gradually increased ( Fig. 5A-C, Fig. S8A-F). Similarly, after the addition of TMX in the system, the catalytic activity of MQD@MIP was inhibited, the AuNP generated in the system was reduced, and the value of ∆A550 nm gradually decreased (Fig. 5D). Therefore, a new UV-Abs method for detection of TMX could be established. Similarly, we also studied MIP/CD@MIP/MQD@NIP/MQD@MIPTMX/MIPTMX recognition effect on TMX (Fig. 5E-F, Fig. S8G-H). Generally speaking, with the increase of AuNP particle size, the Abs peak will redshift, and the peak width changes due to the difference particle size distribution.

A: 50 μg/mL HAuCl4-100 μmol/L HCl-0.75 g/L MA-MQD@MIP (0, 0.05, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0, 1.25, 1.5) μg/mL; B: 50 μg/mL HAuCl4-100 μmol/L HCl-0.75 g/L MA-MQD (0, 0.04, 0.006, 0.12, 0.16, 0.2, 0.24) μg/mL; C: 50 μg/mL HAuCl4-100 μmol/L HCl-0.75 g/L MA-MIP (0, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5) μg/mL; D: 1.0 μg/mL MQD@MIP-50 μg/mL HAuCl4-100 μmol/L HCl-0.75 g/L MA-TMX (0, 5.0, 10, 20, 50, 100, 125) nmol/L; E: 1.0 μg/mL MIP-50 μg/mL HAuCl4-100 μmol/L HCl-0.75 g/L MA-TMX (0, 15, 25, 50, 75, 100, 150) nmol/L; F: 1.0 μg/mL CD@MIP-50 μg/mL HAuCl4-100 μmol/L HCl-0.75 g/L MA-TMX (0, 7.5, 10, 25, 50, 100, 150) nmol/L.

Under experimental conditions, MQD@MIP could catalyze the HAuCl4-MA indicator reaction to generate AuNPs. In a certain concentration range, with the increase of MQD@MIP concentration, the spectral signal had a linear relationship with the generated AuNPs. In the presence of VB4r, AuNP had the strongest SERS peak at 1617 cm−1, and the increased value of SERS signal had a linear relationship with the catalyst concentration. In order to investigate the catalytic ability of MQD@MIP, MQD, MIP, MQD@NIP, MQD@MIPTMX and MIPTMX, the slope K of their catalytic indicator reaction was compared. It can be seen from Table S1 that KMQD＞KMQD@MIP＞KMQD@MIPTMX＞KMIP＞KMQD@NIP＞KMIPTMX, we know that the higher the slope, the greater the catalytic capacity [32]. Because the particle size of MQD was 3.7 nm, it had the smallest particle size and the strongest catalytic capacity (KMQD=11419) with rich surface electrons (π). That could accelerate the nanoredox electron transfer. When MQD was coated by MIP to form the core-shell structure MQD@MIP nanoprobe, the exposed sites of MQDs were reduced and the particle size was enlarged (Fig. 2E). Therefore, its catalytic capacity was greatly reduced (KMQD@MIP=4215.3). Due to water elution of TMX, only limited TMX was elided from MQD@MIPTMX and almost no MQD was exposed. It may be because the added TMX contains multiple N elements, N lone pair electrons and CN, N = O double bonds to form a larger π bond system. π electrons can be coupled with MQD@MIP nano surface electrons. Therefore, the catalytic activity of MQD@MIPTMX was stronger than that of MIP. After TMX and MQD@MIP were stationary for a period of time, it was found that the signal intensity of ∆I1617 cm-1, ∆I370 nm and ∆A550 nm showed a linear decrease. The reason was that the TMX-specific recognition sites on the surface of MQD@MIP were occupied by TMX, when TMXs were added. It hindered the contact between the internal MQD and the external reaction system, leading to the decrease of its catalytic ability and the reduction of AuNPs. So, MQD@MIP nanoprobe can be used as catalyst and as recognition element for the dual-function nanoenzyme. At the same time, MQD@MIP was used as the RRS probe to directly study the interaction between MQD@MIP and TMX, and it was found that there was no significant change in RRS signal after combining TMX.

MA/fumaric acid (FA) could reduce HAuCl4 to generate AuNPs (Fig. 4A, S9A), while succinic acid (SA) could not reduce Au3+ (Fig. S9B). At the same time, it is found that oxalic acid (OA) can reduce Au3+ to generate AuNPs without catalyst under the same conditions (Fig. S9C), which shows that OA has strong reducibility. In the experiment, the intermediate and final products of HAuCl4-HCl-MA reaction were obtained by centrifugation 10 min with 100 rpm, and its FTIR spectra were recorded (Fig. S10A). It was found that there was an obvious peak at 3436 cm−1 of the end product, which was the O-H stretching peak, and the wide peak shape was usually due to the stretching vibration of hydroxyl group of carboxylic acid. The double bond stretching vibration peak appeared at 1635 cm−1. Therefore, it could be concluded that the final product of MA reduction may be OA and finally oxidized to CO2 and H2O. We know the relative standard electrode potential of Au covalent state, E0 (Au3+/Au+) = 1.401 V, E0 (Au+/Au0) = 1.692 V. Therefore, Au3+ has a low redox potential and it is difficult to be reduced to Au0 in one step. As shown in Fig. S10B (a, b), under the same conditions, both Au3+ and Au+ can be reduced to Au0 by MA. More importantly, MQD@MIP can promote Au+ to generate more Au0, accelerate Au3+ to generate Au+, and then reduce Au+ to Au0 in Fig. S10B (c, d). Therefore, the nanoenzyme could catalyze the redox reaction, to enhance the electrons transfer from Au3+ to Au, to generate more AuNPs. Therefore, MQD@MIP nanoprobe could greatly promote the MA-Au3+ nanogold indicator reaction, and further accelerated the transfer of electrons in the redox reaction. Au3+ was reduced to Au0, MA was oxidized to OA, and finally oxidized to CO2 and H2O to generate more AuNPs in the system ( Fig. 6). The main reactions were as follows,
(1)

2
O
(5)
nAu → AuNP

Under the optimal experimental conditions (Fig. S11-S14), the concentration of TMX had a good linear relationship with the SERS/RRS/Abs signal. The working curve is drawn based on TMX concentration and corresponding ∆I/∆A, and the analysis characteristics shows in Table 1. The slopes of SERS were largest among SERS, RRS and Abs, which indicated that SERS was most sensitive. It could be seen from Table 1 that the slope of the MQD@MIP system was greater than that of other system, which indicated that the system was more sensitive. The standard deviation (Sd) was obtained by parallel measurement of blank samples for 11 times, and the limits of detection (LOD) and limits of quantification (LOQ) were calculated according to the formula LOD= 3 ×Sd/N (N is the slope of working curve) and LOQ= 10 ×Sd/N, respectively. At the same time, the comparison of the specific recognition of SMIPs prepared from different substrates showed that the LOD of MQD@MIP specific recognition was the lowest (Table 1). Compared with reported methods for TMX detection (Table S2), it can be seen that this method has high sensitivity and good selectivity.

According to the experimental method, the influence of the coexisting substances of the TMX-MQD@MIP-HAuCl4–HCl-MA systems on the determination of 75 nmol/L TMX was investigated by SERS spectroscopy. 500 times of dinotefuran, atrazine, imidacloprid, thiacloprid, K+, Na+, SO4 2-, NO2 -, CO3 2-, Cu2+, Ba2+, Zn2+, HCO3 2-, Hg+, F+ and Al3+, 450 times of acetamiprid and NH4 +, and 350 times of Fe3+ had no interference on the SERS determination of TMX. The experimental results showed that the RSD was within ± 10 % and common substances with similar structures and ions had no effect on the measurement of TMX. The results showed that this method had good selectivity.

Leaf vegetables (pakchoi, Chinese cabbage, celery) were obtained from the local supermarket in Guilin, China. Sample treatment was according to reference [33]. The content of TMX was determined according to the experimental method, RSD (n = 5) was calculated, and the recovery rate was calculated by standard addition method. The experiment shows that the RSD of the SERS system for leaf vegetable samples is 3.2–8.8 %, and the recovery is between 98.1 % and 103 % (Table S3). According to the maximum residue limit (MRL) of Pesticides in Food under the National Standard for Food Safety (GB 2763–2021), the MRL of TMX on leafy vegetables is 1–5 mg/kg. And the TMX content in the measured samples was much lower than the national standard. However, the MRL of TMX in celery is 1 mg/kg. The European Union has banned the use of TMX outdoors, but the use of TMX in celery is still allowed in China. Therefore, an in-depth study on the residual risk of TMX in celery can provide data support for the rational use of TMX in celery.

In this paper, TMX was used as a template molecule to prepare a core-shell structure SMIP nanoprobe (MQD@MIP) with high catalytic amplification and specific recognition function. It could be used as a catalyst to promote the nanogold indicator reaction. When TMX entered the surface imprinted sites of MQD@MIP, its catalytic activity decreased and less AuNPs were generated. Within a certain concentration range of TMX, SERS/RR/Abs signal decreased linearly with the increase of TMX concentration. Therefore, a simple trimode SMIP sensing platform with high sensitivity and selectivity for TMX detection was established. The sensing platform detection of TMX based on the SERS/RRS/Abs signal reduced value after the reaction of MQD@MIP nanoprobe with TMX, and its sensitivity was limited. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop a dual-function SMIP probe with signal value enhancement.

Hongyan Bai: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Guiqing Wen: Data curation. Aihui Liang: Formal analysis, Supervision. Zhiliang Jiang: Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21767004) and the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (No. 2022GXNSFDA035073).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.134032.

ce:section.ce:para:

Actuators B Chem. 319 (2020) 128308. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.128308.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Zirconium(Ⅳ)-based metal-organic framework for determination of imidacloprid and thiamethoxam pesticides from fruits by UPLC-MS/MS
Food Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Xu, X. Li, W. Zhang, H. Jiang, Y. Pu, J. Cao, W. Jiang, Zirconium(Ⅳ)-based metal-organic framework for determination of imidacloprid and thiamethoxam pesticides from fruits by UPLC-MS/MS, Food Chem. 344 (2021) 128650. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2020.128650.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Hongyan Bai, Ph.D. candidate of Guangxi Normal University, majoring in analytical chemistry, research direction: nano-labeled analysis, nanoparticle resonance scattering spectroscopy and spectral analysis, nucleic acid aptamer nano-catalytic spectrum analysis.

Guiqing Wen, a professor of analytical chemistry in school of environment and resource, Guangxi normal university, China. She received her PhD degree in analytical chemistry from Guangxi normal university. Research interests are molecular spectroscopy, nanoprobes and nanocatalysis.

Aihui Liang, a professor of analytical chemistry in school of environment and resource, Guangxi normal university, China. She received her MS degree in chemistry from Guangxi normal university. Her research focuses on molecular spectroscopy and nanocatalysis analysis.

Zhiliang Jiang, a professor of analytical chemistry in school of environment and resource, Guangxi normal university, China. He received his PhD degree in analytical chemistry from southwest normal university. Mainly engaged in the analysis of environmental pollutants and research on new technological methods. He has innovatively combined resonance Rayleigh scattering, surface enhanced Raman scattering spectroscopy quantitative analysis, and other technologies with nanocatalysis and ligand reactions to construct a series of new simple, fast, highly sensitive, and selective molecular spectroscopic analysis methods.
